coredata.dc:title: Modification of C.I.Pigment Blue 15:6 with graphene oxide
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Dongjun
Zhang, Yuexing
Zhang, Xiaolei
Tang, Jing
Li, Xiaolong
Li, Qiuya
Guo, Yongsheng
Zhang, Jiahui
coredata.dc:description: The ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanine is suitable for printing inks, coatings, and plastic colouring due to its strong red-blue tones, and high thermal and chemical stability. And it also shows good photoconductive properties. In this study, C.I. Pigment Blue 15:6 (P.B.15:6) was modified with monolayer graphene oxide (GO) to improve its colour performance. The modified pigments were used as carrier generating materials, and N,N,N′,N′-tetra(4-methylphenyl)-1,1′-biphenyl-4,4′-diamine as hole transport material to fabricate laser organic photoconductive (OPC) devices, and the photosensitivity was tested. The testes results showed that the modified pigment exhibited higher tinting strength (108%). Besides, the photoconductive properties of OPC devices prepared with modified P.B.15:6 at 650 nm and 780 nm lasers were superior to those of the unmodified P.B.15:6 devices. And when the GO modification amount was 3%, the device shows an outstanding initial surface potential (−650 V) and good photosensitivity (0.218 μJ/cm2). Strong interactions between GOc molecule and CuPc molecules have been shown by calculations based on density-functional theory, which facilitate the charge transfer from the GO to the CuPc010 surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Copper phthalocyanine
C.I. pigment blue 15:6
Graphene oxide
Modified
Photoconductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Graphene oxide modification of pigment blue 15:6
Crystallographic stability of graphene oxide modified pigment blue 15:6
Fluorescence spectra of modified pigment blue 15:6
Particle size distribution analysis of pigment blue 15:6
Atomic force microscopy (AFM) three-dimensional (3D) morphology of modified and un-modified P.B.15:6
Photoconductive properties of GO modified C.I. Pigment blue 15:6
Investigation adsorption of GO on CuPc

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of graphene oxide modified P.B.15:6.
XRD pattern of P.B.15:6 before and after graphene oxide modification.
Fluorescence spectra of P.B.15:6 and different additions of GO modified P.B.15:6.
XPS spectrum of P.B.15:6 and GO-modified P.B.15:6.
C 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of P.B.15:6 (a) and GO-modified P.B.15:6 (b). N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of P.B.15:6 (c) and GO-modified P.B.15:6 (d).
Particle size distribution of Pigment Blue 15:6 before and after modification.
Particle size distribution of Pigment Blue 15:6 and GO modified pigment blue after grinding.
Three-dimensional micromorphology of P.B.15:6@PVB films: (a) unmodified, (b) 2% GO, (c) 3% GO, and (d) 5% GO.

The photocurrent induction curves (a) and dark decay curves (b) for organic photoconductive devices prepared using P.B.15:6 nanoparticles as carrier current generating material before and after GO modification under 650 nm laser beam and photocurrent induction curves (c) and dark decay curves (d) under 780 nm laser beam.
Optimized geometrical structure of the adsorption of GO onto CuPc010. The central orange, blue, white, and gray spheres represent copper, nitrogen, hydrogen, and carbon atoms, respectively.
The electron density map of the adsorption of GO onto CuPc010.
Colour properties of monolayer graphene oxide modified P.B.15:6.
Particle size distribution before and after Pigment Blue 15:6 modification.
Particle size distribution for Pigment Blue 15:6 after grinding.
Photoelectric performance data of P.B.15:6 devices without modification and GO modification under 650 nm laser beam.
Photoelectric performance data of P.B.15:6 devices without modification and GO modification under 780 nm laser beam.

head.ce:title: Modification of C.I.Pigment Blue 15:6 with graphene oxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Monocrystalline Silicon Semiconductor Materials and Technology, Dezhou, 253023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Monocrystalline Silicon Semiconductor Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Monocrystalline Silicon Semiconductor Materials and Technology, Dezhou 253023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. De Zhou University, Dezhou, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanine is suitable for printing inks, coatings, and plastic colouring due to its strong red-blue tones, and high thermal and chemical stability. And it also shows good photoconductive properties. In this study, C.I. Pigment Blue 15:6 (P.B.15:6) was modified with monolayer graphene oxide (GO) to improve its colour performance. The modified pigments were used as carrier generating materials, and N,N,N′,N′-tetra(4-methylphenyl)-1,1′-biphenyl-4,4′-diamine as hole transport material to fabricate laser organic photoconductive (OPC) devices, and the photosensitivity was tested. The testes results showed that the modified pigment exhibited higher tinting strength (108%). Besides, the photoconductive properties of OPC devices prepared with modified P.B.15:6 at 650 nm and 780 nm lasers were superior to those of the unmodified P.B.15:6 devices. And when the GO modification amount was 3%, the device shows an outstanding initial surface potential (−650 V) and good photosensitivity (0.218 μJ/cm2). Strong interactions between GOc molecule and CuPc molecules have been shown by calculations based on density-functional theory, which facilitate the charge transfer from the GO to the CuPc010 surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the symmetry, planarity, and overall electron conjugation properties of molecule structure, the phthalocyanine shows excellent chemical stability and weather fastness. It can be used not only as an essential pigment but also as significant functional material due to its unique photoconductivity and electron transfer properties [1–5]. Copper phthalocyanine pigments are typically compounds that exhibit homogeneous hetero-crystalline phenomena. Various crystalline structures can be obtained, such as α, β, γ, and ε crystalline types [6] depending on the different molecular crystallization and lattice arrangements. The ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanine possesses the strongest red-blue tones, high thermal and chemical stability. It is widely used in the fields of printing inks, coatings, plastic colouring and photoconductive materials. As a functional pigment, it is used in electrostatic copying material, liquid crystal colour filter [7], organic photoconductive material [8], and solar cell [9,10]. It is primarily utilized in the charge-generating layer of electrostatic copying as a charge-generating material for multi-layer organic photoconductors [11]. In the application of ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanine as a photoconductive material [12–14], the coupling between the molecules within the crystal is strong, facilitating an effortless transfer of charges and resulting in a high photosensitivity [15–17].

Graphene oxide (GO) is a graphene derivative with high oxygen content. Due to the high electronegativity of oxygen atoms relative to carbon atoms, GO can be regarded as a p-type doped semiconductor material [18,19]. In GO, the surface of carbon atoms is bonded to oxygenic groups such as hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl [20,21], and several carbon atoms are converted from sp2 to sp3 hybridization. The π-bonds arising from the partially sp2-hybridized carbon atoms confer the GO of electrical conductivity, while the δ-bonds generated by sp3 hybridization give rise to a certain forbidden bandwidth. The energy band structure of the whole GO, including the position of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and the bandgap width, are determined by the relative proportions of sp3 hybridization and sp2 hybridization [21]. Due to the planar structures of P.B.15:6 and GO, they can form non-covalent complexes through π-π stacking interactions. And they can form an electron donor-acceptor (D-A) system, with P.B.15:6 acting as the electron donor and GO acting as the electron acceptor. This compound enhances the dispersion of GO on the surface of P.B.15:6 and maintains its crystalline structure. Additionally, it promotes further separation of graphene flakes and preserves the original morphology. Therefore, the non-covalent composition of GO and P.B.15:6 is not only beneficial for the separation of photogenerated charge carriers generated by P.B.15:6 but also facilitates the absorption of incident light by the photoelectric composite material formed by both components, thus improving the photosensitivity of organic photoconductor.

In our previous work, the graphene was used to modify phthalocyanine blue B (α-crystalline) and phthalocyanine blue BGS (β-crystalline). The phthalocyanine blue B and BGS modified with monolayer GO exhibited a narrower particle size distribution, and higher colouring strength [22]. In this study, the monolayer GO was used to modify P.B.15:6, aiming to enhance its colour performance. Additionally, the modified composite pigment was utilized as charge-generating materials, and N,N,N′,N′-tetra(4-methylphenyl)-1,1′-biphenyl-4,4′-diamine (S100) was used as a hole transport material to fabricate laser organic photodetectors, thereby improving their photosensitivity. Finally, a system model was established using P.B.15:6 and GO by a computational method based on density functional theory (DFT) based first-principles. The adsorption structure and electronic properties of GO on the surface of P.B.15:6 molecules and the interaction behaviour were investigated. Fig. 1 illustrates the preparation process of graphene oxide modified P.B.15:6.

The powder crystal form of P.B.15:6 and modified pigments were analysed using X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D8A, Bruker, Germany), and the testing was conducted using a Cu target, with a scanning voltage of 40 kV and a scanning current of 15 mA. The scanning range was set from 5 to 35°, with a scanning speed of 7°/min and a step size of 0.02°. The colour performance of pigment samples was tested following the protocol specified in the Chinese standard GB/T 1708-79, and the colour performance parameters were obtained using a spectrophotometer (X-RITE 8400, USA). The particle size and distribution of pigment samples were measured using a laser nanoparticle size analyser (Mastersizer 2000, Malvern Instruments, UK), and the particle size and distribution of P.B.15:6 in the dispersion were measured using a Nanotrac Wave II nanoparticle sizer (McKeek, USA). The solid-state fluorescence spectra were measured using a fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980, Edinburgh, UK). The XPS photoelectron spectroscopy was conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer using Al Kα (wavelength 1486.6 eV) radiation with a passage energy of 30 eV. The surface morphology of P.B.15:6 dispersion was characterized using an atomic force microscope (Dimension Icon, Bruker, Germany), and the test samples were prepared by depositing 2–3 drops of dispersed solution onto a glass slide (2 × 2 cm2), rotating at 2000 r/min, and drying at 80 °C. The photoconductivity performance of the Organic Photoconductor (OPC) device was measured using a digitally modified SP-428 electrostatic paper analyser (Kawaguchi Electric Co., Ltd., Japan) to obtain the photocurrent discharge curves with monochromatic laser wavelengths of 650 nm and 780 nm.

Monolayer graphene oxide (GO) powder was purchased from Tianjin Pulan Nanotechnology Company Limited, and C.I.Pigment Blue 15:6 (P.B.15:6) was obtained from Hebei MeiLida Co., Ltd. All other reagents were of analytical grade and were not subjected to further purification.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of P.B.15:6 was measured according to the established method [23,24]. Using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54, in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4) as a standard, the quantum yield of P.B.15:6 was estimated. The QY of P.B.15:6 was evaluated by comparing their integrated fluorescence intensity and the absorbance value (less than 0.1) with quinine sulfate. The QY of P.B.15:6 was calculated according to the equation:
Ys = Yr (Fs/Fr) (Ar/As) (ns/nr)2
where Y is the QY and F is the measured integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, n is the refractive index of the solvent (nr = 1.428, ns = 1.478). The subscript “r” and “s” are the standard and the sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene oxide modification of pigment blue 15:6
ce:section.ce:para: The monolayer GO powder was added to pure water to form a 0.005 g/mL dispersion, which was homogenized by sonication. The P.B.15:6 powder (0.6 g) was slowly added to a biphasic mixture of o-dichlorobenzene (50 mL)/water (50 mL) emulsion and stirred for 30 min. GO dispersions with concentrations of 2%, 3%, 5%, and 10% of P.B.15:6 wt were added, and the mixture was continuously stirred under strong mechanical agitation while gradually raising the temperature to 45 °C and kept for 4 h. Then it was allowed to settle and separated into blue organic and colourless aqueous phases. The organic phase was repeatedly extracted with deionized water until the conductivity of water phase was less than 5 μS/cm. Methanol was added to the organic phase, and when the P.B.15:6 precipitated and settled from the mixture, and after filtration through a G4 sand core funnel, the filter cake was washed with methanol and deionized water in turn until the filtrate conductivity was less than 5 μS/cm. Following vacuum freeze drying, a bright blue modified pigment sample was obtained.

Undercoat Layer: The polyimide (2.5 g) was dissolved in methanol (50 mL) to prepare a pre-coating solution. The solution was dip-coated onto a 100 × 100 mm aluminium foil substrate and dried at 60 °C to obtain an undercoat layer.

Charge Generation Layer: The polyvinyl butyral (0.2 g) was dissolved in a mixture of butanone (18 mL) and cyclohexanone (3 mL), and then P.B.15:6 (0.2 g) and zirconium beads (100 g) were added. The obtained mixture was ball-milled for 2 h and filtered through a G3 glass sand core funnel to obtain a blue P.B.15:6 dispersion. The dispersion was dip-coated onto the pre-coating layer and dried away from light to obtain an ε-CuPc charge generation layer.

Hole transporting layer: The polycarbonate (10 g) and S100 (10 g) were dissolved in 1,2-dichloroethane (100 mL) to make a cavity transfer layer coating solution. The OPC devices with a bifunctional layer structure were obtained by dip-coating them on top of CGL and drying at 120 °C [25].

Due to the low quantity of GO, a cluster model containing six benzene rings with the edge saturated with hydrogen and one non-edge carbon replaced with oxygen was used (GOc). The 010 surface of CuPc crystal (CuPc010) were cleaved and 50 Å thick vacuum layer were then added to avoid surface the interaction between the cleaved layers. GOc was put on the CuPc010 surfaces to obtain the GOc@CuPc010 models. Structure optimization and electron property calculations were performed adopting Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) [26] exchange correlation functional of generalized gradient approximation (GGA) with Grimme's default DFT-D parameters [27]. The double numeric with polarization functions basis set (DNP) was chosen while the core electrons were treated using Effective Core Potentials (ECP). The convergence criteria were set to: (a) an SCF tolerance of 1 × 10−5 Hartree, (b) an energy tolerance of 2 × 10−5 Hartree, (c) maximum force tolerance of 2 × 10−3 Hartree/Å, and (d) maximum displacement tolerance of 5 × 10−3 Å, (e) thermal smearing of 0.010 Ha, (f) Monkhorst-Pack grid k-points of 1 × 1 × 1. All DFT calculations were performed using the DMol3 module of BIOVIA Materials Studio 2017 (17.2). Complexation energy (Ec) was calculated by Ec = E(total) - E(part1) -E(part2) where E(total) is the electronic energy of each model system while E(part1) and E(part2) is the electronic energy of GOc and the remaining part of the model system [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystallographic stability of graphene oxide modified pigment blue 15:6
ce:section.ce:para: Effects of the addition of GO on the crystalline of P.B.15:6 are investigated using X-ray powder diffraction, and the results are shown in Fig. 2 .

During the induction of ε-crystalline formation in a W/O medium formed by o-dichlorobenzene and water, GO was added to modify P.B.15:6, and the role of o-dichlorobenzene is to induce rearrangement of the P.B.15:6 molecule within the crystal to form a specific seed crystal [29].

As shown in Fig. 2, the typical characteristic diffraction peaks of ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanines are observed at 2θ = 7.7°, 9.2°, 17.5°, 17.9°, 22.0°, 23.6°, 28.4°, and 30.3°, and the corresponding crystal plane spacings can be calculated from the Bragg equation to be 11.49, 9.56, 5.04, 4.94, 4.03, 3.76, 3.14 and 2.94 Å. This result is consistent with the reported crystalline surface spacing parameters of ε-crystalline copper phthalocyanine [30,31]. In addition, it is confirmed that the crystalline shape of P.B.15:6 did not change after the 2%, 3%, 5% and 10% GO modification, and the diffraction curves and diffraction peak positions of modified P.B.15:6 remains the same ε-crystalline form as that of unmodified P.B.15:6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectra of modified pigment blue 15:6
ce:section.ce:para: The solid-state fluorescence spectra of P.B.15:6 modified with 2%, 3%, 5%, and 10% GO were measured with an excitation wavelength of 324 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 3 .

It is found that the P.B.15:6 exhibits fluorescence emission which maximizes at 470 nm, and the quantum yield is estimated to be 1.41% using quinine sulfate as the reference. With the increasement in the GO addition amount, the intensity of this emission peak and quantum yield gradually decrease [32,33]. When the GO addition amount is 10%, the fluorescence intensity of modified P.B.15:6 decreased by approximately 80% and the quantum yield dropped to 1.19%. The fluorescence quenching caused by the GO modification indicates that the energy of the phthalocyanine pigment is more efficiently transferred in a non-radiative form after GO modification. There is a photo-induced transfer of electrons or energy between the P.B. 15:6 and GO [34]. The burst of fluorescence intensity implies that the modified pigment can be used as an active layer material for photoelectric conversion devices.

The X-ray photoelectron spectra of P.B.15:6 before and after the modification are shown in Fig. 4 . Four peaks can be observed in the XPS full spectrum, and the peaks with binding energies of 936 eV, 533 eV, 400 eV, and 283 eV are corresponding to the Cu 2p, O 1s, N 1s and C 1s characteristic peak, respectively.

As shown in the C 1s split peak diagram of P.B.15:6 in Fig. 5 a and b, the peaks with binding energies of 285.18 eV and 286.23 eV are attributed to the aromatic rings C=C and C-N, respectively. The C=C and C-N characteristic peaks of GO-modified P.B.15:6 are shifted downwards by 0.3 eV and 0.65 eV, respectively.

The peaks with binding energies of 398.68 eV and 401.63 eV are attributed to N=C and N-Cu, respectively, as shown in the N 1s high-resolution spectrum of P.B.15:6 in Fig. 5c and d. The binding energies of the N=C and N-Cu characteristic peaks of the GO modified P.B.15:6 are shifted downwards by 0.25 eV and 1.35 eV. The peak shift indicates that the charge transfer between P.B.15:6 and g GO occurred via Cu and N atoms [35]. And the P.B.15:6 and GO are bonded to each other through non-covalent bonds.
The colour properties of the pigments before and after modification with 2%–10% GO are tested, and the results are shown in Table 1 .

It can be seen that the modified pigments showed negative ΔL and Δc values, indicating that the GO modification decreases the lightness and colour saturation of P.B.15:6. Additionally, the GO modification can make the modified pigment shifts from greenish-blue to reddish-blue, which is presented by the positive value of Δa and Δh. For the tinting strength, the modified pigments exhibit 3%–8% higher tinting strength than the unmodified pigments, and the P.B.15:6 modified with 5% GO shows the highest tinting strength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size distribution analysis of pigment blue 15:6
ce:section.ce:para: The particle size distribution of organic pigments is an important characteristic that affects their colour light, mobility, and tinting strength [36]. Therefore, the particle size and distribution of pigment samples before and after GO modification are analysed using a laser particle size analyser, and the results are shown in Fig. 6 and Table 2 .

Fig. 6 shows that the particle size distribution of modified P.B.15:6 is significantly narrower than that of unmodified P.B.15:6. Table 2 shows that the small particle size D10 and medium particle size D50 of the modified pigments are not significantly different from those of unmodified P.B.15:6. However, the large particle size D90 is significantly reduced, and the P.B.15:6 modified with 5%GO shows the most significant decrease in large particle size values.

The unmodified, 2%, 3%, and 5% GO-modified P.B.15:6 were further subjected to continuous ball milling using a mixture of butanone and cyclohexanone with Φ1mm zirconium beads for 2 h. The particle size distribution of P.B.15:6 in butanone and cyclohexanone dispersion was measured using the dynamic light scattering principle. The results are shown in Fig. 7 and Table 3 .

As shown in Table 3, the average particle size of P.B.15:6 after grinding is 0.0891 μm, while the average particle sizes of the 2%, 3%, and 5% GO modified C.I. Pigment Blue 15:6 after grinding are 0.0883, 0.0854 and 0.0712 μm, respectively. The GO-modified P.B.15:6 dispersion has a smaller particle size and a narrower concentrated particle size distribution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Atomic force microscopy (AFM) three-dimensional (3D) morphology of modified and un-modified P.B.15:6
ce:section.ce:para: P.B.15:6/PVB, P.B.15:6–2%GO/PVB, P.B.15:6–3%GO/PVB, and P.B.15:6–5%GO/PVB dispersions were prepared. After dilution to an appropriate concentration, the obtained dispersion was spin-coated onto glass slides and dried. The AFM 3D morphology of the P.B.15:6 pigment particles was obtained as shown in Fig. 8 . The scan ranges were 2.5 × 2.5 μm2, and the observation scales were 142.7 nm, 81.5 nm, 61.1 nm, and 48.0 nm, respectively. The P.B.15:6 pigment particles in the three figures are in an 'island' single-particle state, with roughness Rq of 38.8 nm for unmodified pigment (a), 20.5 nm for 2% GO modified (b), 19.5 nm for 3% GO modified (c) and 13.4 nm for 5% GO modified (d) pigments, respectively. It indicates that the GO modification results in smaller particle size and flatter the P.B.15:6 carrier-generating layer films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoconductive properties of GO modified C.I. Pigment blue 15:6
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the results of the particle size, crystalline stability, and dispersion stability studies, the unmodified P.B.15:6 and modified P.B.15:6 with 2%, 3%, and 5% GO were used as carrier-generating materials to prepare laser photoconductive devices. The photo discharge and dark decay curves are shown in Fig. 9 . The photoconductive properties of the devices are shown in Tables 4 and 5
.

It can be observed that the photoconductive properties of OPC devices prepared using GO-modified P.B.15:6 are significantly better than that prepared using unmodified P.B.15:6 at 650 nm and 780 nm laser beams. The 3% GO modification shows excellent initial surface potential (−651 V, −650 V) and photosensitivity (0.281 μJ/cm2, 0.218 μJ/cm2), while the 5% GO modification exhibited slightly lower performance with photosensitivity of 0.328 and 0.234 μJ/cm2. What obtained above shows that the GO-modification can effectively eliminate surface defects to improve dispersion, enhance crystalline stability by blocking the solvent-induced effect and mechanical forces. Furthermore, it facilitates the separation of photogenerated charge carriers generated by P.B.15:6 and promotes the absorption of incident light by the photoelectronic composite material formed by both components. As a result, the optoelectronic conductivity of the device is significantly improved. In addition, the smaller particle size and larger the specific surface, effectively increases the number of electron-hole pairs produced per unit mass of P.B.15:6 and the contact area between the pigment particles and carrier transport material. This facilitates the migration of free carriers and improves the photosensitivity of P.B.15:6.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation adsorption of GO on CuPc
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 10 shows the optimized structure of GOc@CuPc010. GOc takes herringbone structure with CuPc010 surface. One GOc can interact with two more CuPc molecules with the shortest distance of only 2.755 Å. GOc has strong interactions with both CuPc010 surfaces with an interaction energy of −1.31 eV. Electron is found to transfer from GOc to Pc surfaces, with the value of 0.245 e for CuPc010 surfaces. The shorter interaction distance in GOc@CuPc010 explains the slightly larger interaction energy of GOc@CuPc010. The electron density difference map in Fig. 11 clearly shows their different interaction model and electron transfer extent.

By using a monolayer GO modification, the modified P.B.15:6 shows a smaller particle size and narrower particle size distribution, and it showed higher tinting strength than the unmodified pigment by 8%. Laser OPC materials prepared using 3% GO-modified P.B.15:6 pigment demonstrated excellent photoconductive properties. In conclusion, the colour proformance and organic photoconductive properties of phthalocyanine pigments are improved by GO-modification. DFT calculations indicate that the interact distance between single GOc molecule and two more CuPc molecules is only 2.755 Å, with a binding energy of −1.31 eV and a transfer charge of 0.245 e, for the transfer of electrons from the GO to the surface of CuPc010.

Dongjun Lv: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing. Yuexing Zhang: Methodology, Software. Xiaolei Zhang: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Jing Tang: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaolong Li: Conceptualization. Qiuya Li: Investigation. Yongsheng Guo: Formal analysis. Jiahui Zhang: Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.
The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2020MB134 and ZR2020QB082), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22008147), TaiShan Industrial Experts program of Shandong Province, China (No.tscx 202211094), the Special Found for major Science and Technology Innovation Projects of DeZhou, China (No. Dk 202318) and Foundation of Dezhou University, China (2020xjrc106, 2019xjrc306 and 2021xjrc305).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phthalocyanines: from outstanding electronic properties to emerging applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Claessens CG, Hahn U, Torres T. Phthalocyanines: From outstanding electronic properties to emerging applications. Chem Rec 2008;8(2):75-97. https://doi.org/10.1002/tcr.20139.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phthalocyanines: an old dog can still have new (Photo)Tricks

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solution-processable silicon phthalocyanines in electroluminescent and photovoltaic devices
